coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent enzyme-linked immunoassay based on silane-doped carbon dots for sensitive detection of microcystin-LR in water and crucian samples
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zhen-Lin
Ye, Song-Ling
Luo, Lin
Hua, Xiude
Lai, Jing-Xian
Cai, Xiang-Ping
Liang, Qing-Wen
Lei, Hong-Tao
Sun, Yuan-Ming
Chen, Yi-ping
Shen, Xing
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a fluorescent nanoparticles labeling-free fluorescence enzyme-linked immunoassay (FELISA) has been established for the ultrasensitive detection of microcystin-LR (MC-LR) in water and fish samples. Polyclonal antibody against MC-LR was labeled with horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and used as signal probe for binding with analyte in sample or for coating antigen. After washing of the unbound antibody, the substrate system (2,2′-azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate) (ABTS)/H2O2) was added. The oxidation product of ABTS (ox-ABTS) catalyzed by HRP effectively caused the fluorescence quenching of subsequently added silane-doped carbon dots (Si-CDs), and the change in fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs was used to realize the quantitative detection of MC-LR. Under the optimum conditions, the Si-CDs based FELISA method showed a good linear relationship from 0.001 to 3.20 μg L−1 (R2 = 0.994) and provided a low detection limit of 0.6 ng L-1, which was approximately 30-fold lower than that of traditional indirect competitive ELISA. Average recovery values from 79.9% to 109.2% was obtained from spiked water and crucian samples, suggesting its potential application on the monitoring of MR-LR at a trace level.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microcystin-LR
Fluorescent enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay
Silane carbon dots
2,2′-azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of HRP conjugate of MC-LR antibody
Characterization of HRP conjugate of MC-LR antibody
Fluorescence quenching of Si-CDs in the HRP-H2O2-ABTS catalytic system
Determination of MC-LR in water and crucian samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Si-CDs. (A) TEM image of the Si-CDs. Inset shows the HRTEM of the Si-CDs. (B) Diameter distribution of histogram of the Si-CDs. (C) FTIR spectrum of the Si-CDs. (D) Absorption (green), fluorescence excitation (black), and emission (red) spectra of the Si-CDs in aqueous solution. Inset shows the corresponding photographs under ambient (left) and 365 nm light (right), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
High resolution XPS spectra of Si-CDs. (A) Full scan, (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, (D) O 1s, (E) Si 2p peak of the Si-CDs.

Characterization of Fluorescence Quenching of Si-CDs in the HRP-H2O2-ABTS Catalytic System. (A) Fluorescence spectra of Si-CDs (black line), the mixture of Si-CDs, 100 mM ABTS and 10 μM H2O2 (blue line), or the mixture of Si-CDs, ox-ABTS produced by H2O2 and 40 pM HRP (red line); (B) The fluorescence spectrum of Si-CDs in the presence of different concentration of HRP; (C) Fluorescence decay times of the detection system in the presence or absence of ox ABTS; (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum of ox-ABTS and fluorescence emission spectrum of Si-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The sensitivity of FELISA and traditional ELISA for MC-LR. (A) The calibration curve of Si-CDs based FELISA for MC-LR (n = 3). (B) The calibration curve of traditional ELISA for MC-LR (n = 3).
The stability of this Si-CDs based FELISA for MC-LR: standard curve of different batches of microplates (A) and different days (B).
Schematic illustration of the Si-CDs based FELISA method for quantitative detection of MC-LR.
Comparison of the developed Si-CDs based FELISA with other immunoassay methods reported for the detection of MC-LR.

row.entry: Magnetic Particle-Based Enzyme Assays and Immunoassays
A silane carbon dots based fluorescent enzyme-linked immunoassay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cross-reactivity of related analogues in the Si-CDs based FELISA.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CR, cross-reactivity. The percentage of CR was calculated using the following equation: CR (%) = [IC50 (MC-LR μg L−1)/IC50 (cross-reactant, μg L−1)] * 100.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of spiked sample analyzed by the Si-CDs based FELISA (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: MC-LR concentration was extrapolated from the calibration curve.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent enzyme-linked immunoassay based on silane-doped carbon dots for sensitive detection of microcystin-LR in water and crucian samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Plant Protection, Nanjing Agricultural University, Nanjing 210095, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Plant Protection, Nanjing Agricultural University, Nanjing 210095, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan 430070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a fluorescent nanoparticles labeling-free fluorescence enzyme-linked immunoassay (FELISA) has been established for the ultrasensitive detection of microcystin-LR (MC-LR) in water and fish samples. Polyclonal antibody against MC-LR was labeled with horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and used as signal probe for binding with analyte in sample or for coating antigen. After washing of the unbound antibody, the substrate system (2,2′-azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate) (ABTS)/H2O2) was added. The oxidation product of ABTS (ox-ABTS) catalyzed by HRP effectively caused the fluorescence quenching of subsequently added silane-doped carbon dots (Si-CDs), and the change in fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs was used to realize the quantitative detection of MC-LR. Under the optimum conditions, the Si-CDs based FELISA method showed a good linear relationship from 0.001 to 3.20 μg L−1 (R2 = 0.994) and provided a low detection limit of 0.6 ng L-1, which was approximately 30-fold lower than that of traditional indirect competitive ELISA. Average recovery values from 79.9% to 109.2% was obtained from spiked water and crucian samples, suggesting its potential application on the monitoring of MR-LR at a trace level.

ce:section.ce:para: Microcystin-LR (MC-LR) is one of the most toxic and well-studied microcystins (Li et al., 2017; Puddick et al., 2014). By inhibiting protein phosphatases (PPs) and causing oxidative stress in eukaryotic cells by reactive oxygen species (ROS), MC-LR present severe toxicity for various organisms and harm humans via drinking water supplies and/ or accumulation in food chains (Campos and Vasconcelos, 2010; Carmichael et al., 2001; Chen et al., 2016; Mcelhiney et al., 2001; Zhang et al., 2009). Moreover, MC-LR was classified as a Class 2B carcinogen by the International Cancer Society (IARC) in 2008. Thus, the World Health Organization (WHO) proposed a provincial upper limit of 1.0 μg L−1 for MC-LR in drinking water and set 0.04 g (kg.d)−1 as a tolerable daily intake (TDI) of MC-LR (WHO, 2003). Therefore, it is essential to establish a rapid detection method for MC-LR in biological samples with high sensitivity and high selectivity.

Up to now, various techniques have been proposed for determination of MC-LR, such as HPLC (Lian et al., 2015; Ma et al.,2015), LC–MS (Qian et al., 2017), capillary electrophoresis (Tong et al., 2010), electrochemical sensors (Du et al., 2016; Gan et al., 2017), biosensors (Li et al., 2016), enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Morenkov et al., 2014), and fluorescence immunoassays (Wu et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2011). In contrast with chromatographic techniques, ELISA has many advantages such as rapidity and low cost, and this method uses the antigen-antibody specific reactions for analyzing the target compound in samples. Many modern chromatographic methods and ELISA can meet the WHO requirements for monitoring MC-LR with limit of 1.0 μg L−1 in real samples. However, the problems of complicated instruments, cumbersome operation and low sensitivity still exist with these methods. For the monitoring of MC-LR, which is commonly found and extremely harmful, 1.0 μg L−1 is not a sufficient limit for analysis. A more sensitive and simpler method is necessary to effectively monitor MC-LR to prevent its harm to humans and the environment.

Fluorescence immunoassays always show higher sensitivity than traditional colorimetry-based ELISA. Moreover, with the development of nanomaterials, fluorescent nanomaterials with excellent performance such as quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs) have increasingly emerged, showing the broad application scope of fluorescent immunosorbent assay. In traditional fluorescence immunoassays, nanomaterials are conjugated with a suitable antigen or antibody. For instance, Yu et al. (2011) adopted QDs-conjugated antibodies as detection probe and immobilized competitive antigens on them to construct a competitive fluorescent immunoassay for MC-LR with a detection limit of 0.03 µg L−1. Wu et al. (2015) coupled CDs with the detection antibody against alpha fetoprotein (AFP) to form a fluorescent detection probe, and coated the capture antibody against AFP on microplate, developing a fluorescent immunoassay for AFP determination. However, the bioconjugation between QDs and antibody inevitably affects the optical property of QDs, and leads to unavoidable loss of antibody activity due to its random orientation. Moreover, as the QDs and CDs are of small size, purification of the resultant antibody-QDs conjugates is a challenging and complicated task (Lin et al., 2009; Luo et al., 2018). Thus, the overall improvement in sensitivity of the established methods is not significant. Therefore, some researchers are aiming to develop fluorescent immunoassays which are free from fluorophore labeling. A fluorophore labeling-free fluorescent ELISA for ultrasensitive detection of zearalenone (ZEN) was developed by Zhan et al. (2016). This method used catalase-mediated fluorescence quenching of CdTe QDs as signal readout, based on the fact that H2O2 can efficiently quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs, and catalase can efficiently catalyze the hydrolysis of H2O2. Nevertheless, the traditional heavy-metals containing QDs are toxic to biological systems, which limit their widespread application in immunoassays (Luo et al., 2018). As zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with particle size of less than 10 nm, CDs have been widely accepted as outstanding alternatives to the toxic heavy metal-containing QDs due to their excellent fluorescence property and good biocompatibility.

In this study, polyclonal antibody against MC-LR labeled with horseradish peroxidase (HRP) was used as signal probe for binding with analyte in sample or coating antigen. It was found that the oxidation product of 2,2′-azinobis-ethyl benzothiazoline-6-sulfonate (ABTS) produced with the aid of HRP and H2O2 effectively quenched the fluorescence signal of silane-doped carbon dots (Si-CDs). This was probably due to dynamic quenching. As is known, HRP is widely used as labeling enzyme in ELISA due to its ease of extraction and its stable nature, and ABTS is one of the most sensitive chromogenic substrates of HRP (Fang et al., 2016). Therefore, a Si-CDs based FELISA using Si-CDs-ABTS-H2O2 as the substrate system was developed for ultrasensitive detection of MC-LR.

The MC-LR standard (95%) was purchased from Algal Scientific, Taiwan, China. Black opaque microporous fluorescent 96-well plates were purchased from Qixiang Biological Technology Co. Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). N-(2-Aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane was obtained from Chenguang Chemical Co. Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), KNO3, KCl, and KH2PO4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St.Louis, USA). 2,2′-Azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate) (ABTS) was obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other solvents and reagents were of analytical grade and purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China).

The fluorescence spectra and values were recorded with a HORIBA QM-40 fluorescence steady-state near-infrared spectrometer (HORIBA, Japan) and a SpectraMax i3x multi-function detection platform (Molecular Devices, USA), respectively. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded by an Evolution 300 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fourier transform-infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was conducted on a VERTEX 70 spectrometer using KBr pellets. The morphologies and sizes of silane CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were carried out using ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray electron spectrometer. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed with a photocounting spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments FLS900, UK).

Hapten-protein conjugates, MC-LR-KLH and MC-LR-BSA were prepared by conjugating MC-LR with corresponding proteins via activated ester method, and used as immunogen and coating antigen, respectively. Further details were presented in Supplementary data.

Animal treatments were following guidelines of the Chinese Association for Laboratory Animal Sciences. Immunogen, MC-LR-KLH was injected into three New Zealand white rabbits (10-wk-old, 1.5–2.0 kg) following described procedures (Luo et al., 2014) to produce Anti-MC-LR polyclonal antibody. Further details were presented in Supplementary data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of HRP conjugate of MC-LR antibody
ce:section.ce:para: The conjugate of HRP with polyclonal anti-MC-LR antibody was synthesized according to the modified version of a simple sodium periodate method (Jung et al., 1989). Firstly, 5 mg HRP was placed into a flask containing 500 μL acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 5.6). Then, 100 μL NaIO4 (0.1 M) was added, and the reaction was stirred at 4 °C for 30 min in the dark. Then, 500 μL of 2.5% ethylene glycol was added dropwise, and the mixture was reacted at room temperature for 30 min. After that, 210 μL of the antibody to be labeled was added dropwise, stirred at 4 °C for 30 min, and dialyzed against carbonate buffer (0.05 M, pH 9.6) overnight. The labeled antibody was taken out the next day, and 100 μL NaBH4 (5 mg mL−1) was added and stirred at 4 °C for 2 h to terminate the reaction. The conjugate was purified by saturated ammonium sulfate and dialyzed against PBS for further use. The HRP labelled antibody was characterized by direct competitive ELISA (Lu et al., 2012).

Si-CDs was synthesized according to a previously reported one-step hydrothermal method with slight modifications (Wang et al., 2011). Briefly, 10 mL N-(2-aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane was placed into a 100 mL three-necked flask, and degassed with nitrogen for 5 min before heating. When the heating temperature reached 240 °C, 0.5 g anhydrous citric acid was quickly added to the solution with vigorous stirring. The mixture was then kept at 240 °C for 1 min. The final products were purified by precipitation with petroleum ether three times. The synthesized Si-CDs were characterized by TEM, XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy.

The schematic diagram of the proposed method is shown in Scheme 1 . The detailed procedure was based on a previous report (Wang et al., 2011). First, 96-well black opaque microplates were coated with 100 μL of MC-LR-BSA in bicarbonate buffer (pH 9.5, 0.05 M) at 37 °C overnight. After being washed twice with washing buffer (PBST, pH 7.4, 0.01 M, containing 0.05% Tween 20), the plates were blocked with blocking buffer (5% skim milk powder dissolved in 1 L water) for 3 h at 37 °C and then dried at 37 °C for 1 h. Then, 50 μL of diluted MC-LR was added to each well followed by adding 50 μL well−1 of diluted enzyme-labeled antibody in PBS. The plates were shaken on a microvibrator for 2 min and incubated in a 37 °C water bath for 50 min. Then, the plates were washed five times to remove unbound HRP-labeled antibody. After that, 100 μL of ABTS substrate solution (PBST buffer (0.01 M, pH 7.4) containing 100 mM ABTS and 10 μM H2O2) was added to the wells and incubated for 20 min at 37 °C. Then, 50 μL of prepared Si-CDs (diluted to 1:100 by water) was added to each well. After the plates were shaken for 2 min, the florescence signals from the Si-CDs were measured (excitation at 370 nm, emission at 470 nm) by the multi-function detection platform (Spectra Max i3x, Molecular Devices, USA).

Tap water was obtained from the laboratory, lake water was taken from the West Lake in the South China Agricultural University, and bottled drinking water was purchased from a local supermarket. For measuring MC-LR content in water, the water samples were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min, filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and diluted 1:2 in PBST buffer (0.01 M, pH 7.4). For measuring MC-LR content in fresh tissue/organ, the crucian samples were purchased from a local market in Changban, Guangzhou, China. Firstly, individual crucian was separated from the skin and bones before extraction. Then, it was minced, crushed and homogenized by a blender before further treatment. Accurately weighed 5.0 g of homogenized tissue or 5.0 g of spiked sample (blank tissue sample which was confirmed by LC–MS/MS (GB5009.273-2016) to be MC-LR free was spiked with MC-LR at different mass ratios) was placed into a 50 mL centrifuge tube and 10 mL of 80% methanol solution was added. After being shaken well for 1 h at room temperature and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant was taken into a new centrifuge tube and the residue was extracted once. The two extracts were combined and made up to 30 mL, passed through glass fiber filters, and diluted 1:10 in PBST buffer (0.01 M, pH 7.4). MC-LR-free samples were used to spike with varying concentrations of MC-LR for recoveries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of HRP conjugate of MC-LR antibody
ce:section.ce:para: All the resulting anti-MC-LR antibodies showed high titer (>1.6 × 104) and certain inhibition with MC-LR (data not shown), especially one anti-MC-LR polyclonal antibody (pAb) which exhibits the highest affinity (IC50 = 0.054 μg L−1, Fig. S1) for MC-LR was used for the further experiments. The glycosyl groups of HRP were oxidized to aldehyde groups through the modified simple sodium periodate method, which could be coupled with amino groups on the anti-MC-LR pAb to form HRP-labeled MC-LR pAb. The obtained antibodies were tested in direct competitive ELISA. It was found that the titer of HRP-labeled MC-LR pAb was about 1:16000, which confirmed the successful coupling of HRP with MC-LR pAb.

The as-synthesized Si-CDs were characterized by TEM, XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 1 , the Si-CDs were well monodispersed and uniform in size. The TEM image in Fig. 1A denotes the representative image of an individual nanoparticle, indicating high crystallinity with a lattice parameter of 0.20 nm, which corresponded to the (1 0 0) diffraction plane of graphitic (sp2) carbon (Dong et al., 2010). As shown in Fig. 1B, the Si-CDs displayed a narrow size distribution (diameter ~1.68–2.97 nm) with an average size of 2.23 nm (n = 100). Furthermore, FT-IR spectra were utilized to further study the surface functional groups present on the as-prepared CDs (Fig. 1C). The absorption bands at 3000–3500 cm−1 and 1700–1740 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of OH, NH, and CO, respectively. The bands at 1136 cm−1 and 1018 cm−1 corresponded to the double peaks of SiOSi and SiOC, indicating that a certain degree of hydrolysis occurred on the silane coated on the surface of the resultant CDs. The UV–Vis spectrum revealed that the aqueous solution of CDs exhibited a maximum absorption peak at 360 nm. In the fluorescence spectra, CDs showed optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 370 nm and 470 nm. The inset in Fig. 1D shows that the Si-CDs water solution was transparent and colorless under natural light, but it emitted strong blue fluorescence under UV light. Furthermore, the quantum yield (QY) of CDs was 14% and the fluorescence lifetime was 2.349 ns.

XPS was used to determine the surface composition and elemental composition of the resultant nanoparticles. The full range XPS analysis (Fig. 2 A) of the Si-CDs indicated four peaks at 284.84, 410.28, 539.58, and 107.28 eV, which were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Si 2p, respectively. High-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2B) confirmed the presence of CC (sp2, 284.6 eV), CO (sp2, 286.4 eV, 287.8 eV), and CN (sp3, 285.6 eV) bonds (Paraknowitsch et al., 2013; Skákalová et al., 2018). This demonstrated the existence of graphitized carbon nuclei and the enrichment of hydrophilic groups on the surface. In Fig. 2C, the presence of N 1s peaks at 398.6 eV and 400.0 eV suggested that nitrogen existed mostly in the form of CNC and N (C)3, respectively (Gong et al., 2009). These data imply that N was partly doped into CDs and silanes were partly coated on CDs. Two peaks at 531.8 eV and 532.8 eV in the XPS spectrum of O 1s were assigned to SiO and CO, respectively (Fig. 2D) (Zhang et al., 2014). Moreover, the detailed XPS spectrum of Si 2p (Fig. 2E) demonstrated the presence of SiC (99 eV, 99.8 eV) and Si-N (101.9 eV) respectively, suggesting that Si was doped into CDs as well (Wen et al., 2014). The overall XPS results were consistent with the FT-IR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of Si-CDs in the HRP-H2O2-ABTS catalytic system
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching of Si-CDs was investigated based on HRP catalyzed oxidization of the chromogenic substrates of ABTS using H2O2 as an oxidant. HRP is a kind of heme-containing protein and widely used in ELISA reactions as a marker enzyme. ABTS is a well-known chromogenic substrate of HRP. From Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity had no significant change upon mixing Si-CDs, ABTS and H2O2 or individual Si-CDs. However, upon adding HRP, the fluorescence intensity was quenched effectively when ABTS was oxidized to ox-ABTS by H2O2 under the catalysis of HRP. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3B, it was obvious that the fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs was quenched progressively with the increasing concentrations of HRP. The response to the concentration of HRP was linear in the range 0 to 100 pM when using ABTS as the substrate of HRP. Fluorescence detection systems can be classified as static or dynamic quenching according to the fluorescence quenching principle. Static quenching results from the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complexes, while dynamic quenching occurs due to the diffusion between the fluorophore and the quencher during the lifetime of the excited state (Cai et al., 2013; Fang et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2014). To further confirm the quenching mechanism, fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted (Fig. 3C). As shown in Table S1, the fluorescence decay curve of free Si-CDs contained only one decay component and the average fluorescence lifetime is 11.32 ns. In comparison, the fluorescence decay curve of Si-CDs in the presence of ox-ABTS change to have dual decay component, and the fluorescence lifetime decreased to 10.64 ns. These results suggested that dynamic quenching occurred in the detection system. Since ox-ABTS is typical electron acceptor and the absorption spectrum (420 nm) of ox-ABTS is overlapped with emission spectrum of Si-CDs (470 nm), meanwhile, an earlier report demonstrated that ABTS has the ability to experience adsorption through hydroxyl groups on carbon based materials contained oxygen containing functionalities (Karnicka et al., 2008), we could conclude that the fluorescence quenching of Si-CDs in presence of ox-ABTS is due to electron transfer or energy transfer (dynamic quenching).

As MC-LR is a relatively small oligopeptide with molecular weight of only 1 kD, it is difficult to apply a sandwich immunoassay for this molecule (Yu et al., 2011). A competitive immunoassay is better for identification of small molecules using a single antibody. The schematic of the competitive FELISA based on fluorescence quenching of Si-CDs is presented in Scheme 1. When the MC-LR molecule was absent from the sample solution, the HRP-labeled MC-LR pAb was captured by MC-LR-BSA which was pre-coated in the microplate. The fluorescence of Si-CDs was quenched effectively because of the ox-ABTS produced from ABTS with the aid of H2O2 and HRP, and a low fluorescence signal was obtained. Conversely, the presence of MC-LR resulted in competitive binding of coated MC-LR-BSA to MC-LR pAb in the microplate. Therefore, less amount of HRP was detected in the microplate, and less amount of Si-CDs was affected by ox-ABTS, resulting in detection of a high fluorescence signal. Consequently, recording the change in fluorescence signals can assist the detection of the analyte.

To achieve the optimum assay performance, several key factors affecting the sensitivity and stability of FELISA were studied, such as the coating concentration of MC-LR-BSA (100, 125, 150, and 250 μg L−1), the species of working solution (H2O, 0.01 M PBST, and 0.01 M PBS), the value of pH (5.4, 6.4, 7.4, 8.4 and 9.4) and different incubation times (20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min). As displayed in Fig. S2, the proposed FELISA showed the best performance for MC-LR when the coating concentration of MC-LR-BSA was 125 μg L−1, concentration of HRP-labeled MC-LR pAb was 9 μg mL−1, the working buffer was 0.01 M PBST with pH value of 7.4 and the incubation time was 50 min. As shown in Fig. 4 , the standard curve exhibited a good linear range from 0.001 μg L−1 to 3.20 μg L−1 with a reliable correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.994). The regression equation could be represented by y = 1.325e6 x + 1.543e7, where y is the fluorescence intensity of silane carbon dots and x is the MC-LR concentration. Error bars were based on three duplicate measurements at different MC-LR concentrations. The IC50 of the FELISA was 0.052 μg L−1 and the LOD (defined as IC10 (Wang et al., 2013)) was calculated to be 0.0006 μg L−1. The LOD of the conventional ELISA was 0.012 μg L−1, which was about 20-fold higher than that of our proposed FELISA. This was because the fluorescence of silane carbon dots was extremely sensitive to the ultrahigh catalytic activity of HRP to H2O2. Therefore, these results confirm that the high performance of carbon dots could be achieved by combining with an optimized competitive immunoassay.

A detailed comparison of the LOD and linearity range values of several reported methods for MC-LR detection is shown in Table 1 . The proposed method shows comparable or better performance than the other reported methods, confirming the feasibility of this method for MC-LR detection.

To determine the specificity of the developed assay, cross-reactivities (CR) with four microcystins with similar structures as MC-LR including Microcystin-RR (MC-RR), Nodularin (NOD), Microcystin-LW (MC-LW), and Microcystin-LF (MC-LF), were measured. As shown in Table 2 , the method showed good specificity to MC-LR, with only slight CR with MC-RR (CR = 15.6%) and NOD (CR = 5.8%), and negligible CR with MC-LW and MC-LF (CR < 0.1%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MC-LR in water and crucian samples
ce:section.ce:para: Matrix effects of different water samples and crucian sample were investigated by using series diluted blank sample extract solutions (in PBST) to construct calibration curves. It is shown in Fig. S3, when water samples (tap water, drinking water and lake water) were diluted 2-fold or higher dilution factors, corresponding calibration curves were nearly overlapped with the calibration curve prepared by PBST. This means that matrix effects of water samples could be minimized to an acceptable level by diluted 2-fold by PBST. For crucian sample, diluting the sample extract 10-fold could eliminate the effect on determination of MC-LR from sample matrices well. Given the dilution factors during sample preparation, the proposed method is still sensitive toward MC-LR in water samples, and the LOD was 1.2 ng L−1 when the water samples were diluted 2 times. Furthermore, the LOD of MC-LR was 36 ng L−1 in crucian samples when they were diluted 60 times.

The accuracy of the method was expressed as recovery rate by analyzing blank samples spiked with different concentrations of standard MC-LR. As indicated in Table 3 , a good recovery percentage in the range of 93.7–109.2% with RSD of 3.8–13.7% was obtained for the spiked water samples and 79.9–107.1% with RSD of 3.2–6.6% for the spiked crucian samples.

The intra-assay and inter-assay variations were used to evaluate the accuracy of the developed method (Fig. 5 ). The inter-assay was performed in five plates coated for different durations with three parallels. The inter-assay was carried out on five consecutive days using the same batch of coated plates. IC50 values were calculated and compared from the dose-response curves. As can be seen, the coefficient of variation (CV) of IC50 was less than 10% in the intra-assay, which indicates that the performance of the coating antigen was stable and the method had good repeatability. On the other hand, when FELISA was performed for five consecutive days under the same optimized experimental conditions, the CV was greater than 10%, indicating that the performance of the coated plates began to change after a few days, and the plates of the same batch should be used within one week as soon as possible. The coefficient of variation for intra-assay and inter-assay using FELISA exhibited acceptable levels of precision for the quantitative analysis of MC-LR. Therefore, the above results indicate that the established FELISA can be used to detect ultralow concentrations of MC-LR in actual environmental water and food samples.

To summarize, highly photoluminescent Si-CDs were first synthesized by a rapid one-step heating method. Then, a novel fluorescence sensing system consisting of Si-CDs/ABTS/H2O2/HRP was constructed for MC-LR detection on the basis of HRP-dependent fluorescence response, since the HRP-mediated oxidation product of ABTS in the presence of H2O2, ox-ABTS, effectively quenched the fluorescence of Si-CDs at 470 nm under excitation at 370 nm. This FELISA method not only has better sensitivity but also avoids the complex bioconjugation step between CDs and antibody, which can greatly broaden the applications of such immunoassays in the fields of food safety monitoring. Thus, the proposed FELISA based on Si-CDs can be a promising platform for the sensitive and high-throughput detection of various food contaminants.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Nature Science Foundation of China (31871833; 31801621), National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFC1601205; 2017YFC1601705), and the Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou (201807010109; 201803020026).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2019.134614.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
